dp.label: dp[i] = LIS length ending at i (default 1)

frames.caption: Base case: every element alone is an increasing subsequence of length 1, so dp is all 1s. Fill dp left to right; each dp[i] looks back at earlier indices.
frames.note: LIS via O(n^2) DP defines dp[i] = length of the longest strictly increasing subsequence ending exactly at index i. Seeding every cell to 1 encodes the "just this element" chain. Filling left to right guarantees that when we compute dp[i], every dp[j] for j < i is already final.

frames.caption: i=0: nums[0]=10. Scan j<0 for nums[j]<10 — no smaller earlier value. dp[0] = 1.
frames.note: dp[i] is the length of the longest increasing subsequence that ENDS at index i. To compute it we look back at every earlier index j: if nums[j] < nums[i], then any chain ending at j can be extended by nums[i], giving a candidate length dp[j] + 1. We keep the best such candidate. A value with no smaller predecessor stays at its default of 1 — it is a chain of just itself.

frames.caption: i=1: nums[1]=9. Scan j<1 for nums[j]<9 — no smaller earlier value. dp[1] = 1.
frames.note: dp[i] is the length of the longest increasing subsequence that ENDS at index i. To compute it we look back at every earlier index j: if nums[j] < nums[i], then any chain ending at j can be extended by nums[i], giving a candidate length dp[j] + 1. We keep the best such candidate. A value with no smaller predecessor stays at its default of 1 — it is a chain of just itself.

frames.caption: i=2: nums[2]=2. Scan j<2 for nums[j]<2 — no smaller earlier value. dp[2] = 1.
frames.note: dp[i] is the length of the longest increasing subsequence that ENDS at index i. To compute it we look back at every earlier index j: if nums[j] < nums[i], then any chain ending at j can be extended by nums[i], giving a candidate length dp[j] + 1. We keep the best such candidate. A value with no smaller predecessor stays at its default of 1 — it is a chain of just itself.

frames.caption: i=3: nums[3]=5. Scan j<3 for nums[j]<5 — smaller earlier values nums[2]=2. dp[3] = 2.
frames.note: Found a strictly smaller earlier value, so we can hang nums[i] onto the chain that ends at j. dp[j] + 1 beats the current dp[i], so we take it. Because dp[j] was already the OPTIMAL chain ending at j (we filled left to right), extending it is optimal too — that is the DP invariant that makes one backward scan per i correct.

frames.caption: i=4: nums[4]=3. Scan j<4 for nums[j]<3 — smaller earlier values nums[2]=2. dp[4] = 2.
frames.note: Found a strictly smaller earlier value, so we can hang nums[i] onto the chain that ends at j. dp[j] + 1 beats the current dp[i], so we take it. Because dp[j] was already the OPTIMAL chain ending at j (we filled left to right), extending it is optimal too — that is the DP invariant that makes one backward scan per i correct.

frames.caption: i=5: nums[5]=7. Scan j<5 for nums[j]<7 — smaller earlier values nums[2]=2, nums[3]=5, nums[4]=3. dp[5] = 3.
frames.note: Found a strictly smaller earlier value, so we can hang nums[i] onto the chain that ends at j. dp[j] + 1 beats the current dp[i], so we take it. Because dp[j] was already the OPTIMAL chain ending at j (we filled left to right), extending it is optimal too — that is the DP invariant that makes one backward scan per i correct.

frames.caption: i=6: nums[6]=101. Scan j<6 for nums[j]<101 — smaller earlier values nums[0]=10, nums[1]=9, nums[2]=2, nums[3]=5, nums[4]=3, nums[5]=7. dp[6] = 4.
frames.note: Found a strictly smaller earlier value, so we can hang nums[i] onto the chain that ends at j. dp[j] + 1 beats the current dp[i], so we take it. Because dp[j] was already the OPTIMAL chain ending at j (we filled left to right), extending it is optimal too — that is the DP invariant that makes one backward scan per i correct.

frames.caption: i=7: nums[7]=18. Scan j<7 for nums[j]<18 — smaller earlier values nums[0]=10, nums[1]=9, nums[2]=2, nums[3]=5, nums[4]=3, nums[5]=7. dp[7] = 4.
frames.note: Found a strictly smaller earlier value, so we can hang nums[i] onto the chain that ends at j. dp[j] + 1 beats the current dp[i], so we take it. Because dp[j] was already the OPTIMAL chain ending at j (we filled left to right), extending it is optimal too — that is the DP invariant that makes one backward scan per i correct.

frames.caption: Answer is max(dp) = 4. The longest increasing subsequence of [10, 9, 2, 5, 3, 7, 101, 18] has length 4 (e.g. 2,3,7,18).
frames.note: Unlike a grid DP, the answer is NOT the last cell: the longest chain can end anywhere, so the answer is max over the whole dp array. Each of the n cells scans up to n predecessors, giving O(n^2) time and O(n) space. The O(n log n) patience/tails method computes the same length faster, but this DP is the one to reach for first in an interview.
